headline: Key Research Findings
subheadline: What we know, what we need to verify, and what it means
toplineTakeaway: 19 Linden St is an exceptional site held back by an aggressive asking price. The investment thesis depends entirely on acquisition price and entitlement strategy.

keyFindings.title: The Location Is Genuinely Rare
keyFindings.detail: A 17,698 sq ft lot in Brookline Village with Green Line access is nearly impossible to replicate. Large parcels in established Brookline neighborhoods almost never come to market. This scarcity is real and defensible.
keyFindings.implication: Supports premium pricing for finished product

keyFindings.title: Historic Status Is a Speed Bump, Not a Wall
keyFindings.detail: The National Register alone imposes zero restrictions on private development. The property is NOT in a Local Historic District. The binding constraint is Brookline's 18-month demolition delay — significant but temporary. After 18 months, the permit must be issued.
keyFindings.implication: Budget for delay costs but don't overweight historic risk

keyFindings.title: As-of-Right Density Cannot Support a $5.95M Acquisition
keyFindings.detail: M-1.0 zoning yields ~14 sellable units on 17,698 sf of GFA. At Boston-area construction costs ($450-550/sf) and Brookline sale prices ($1,000-1,200/sf), the all-in development cost at $5.95M land exceeds or matches achievable revenue.
keyFindings.implication: Must either negotiate price to $4-4.5M or pursue density upside

keyFindings.title: Chapter 40B Could Transform the Economics
keyFindings.detail: A comprehensive permit could allow 25-30 units, nearly doubling the as-of-right density. This dramatically improves per-unit land cost and project margins. However, Brookline is hostile to 40B projects and may have safe harbor protection.
keyFindings.implication: Verify safe harbor status immediately — this is the single highest-impact variable

keyFindings.title: MBTA Communities Act Is a Tailwind
keyFindings.detail: Brookline must zone for 6,990 multifamily units under the MBTA Communities Act. Transit-adjacent parcels like this one may benefit from overlay districts with more generous density. Compliance zoning was adopted in Fall 2023 — check if this parcel is covered.
keyFindings.implication: Could unlock density without the political cost of 40B

keyFindings.title: New Construction Commands Strong Pricing
keyFindings.detail: Recent Brookline new construction sells at $950-$1,400/sf. Median resale at $830/sf with 29% YoY appreciation. The market supports premium product — especially near transit and medical institutions.
keyFindings.implication: Revenue assumptions of $1,100-$1,200/sf are conservative and defensible

keyFindings.title: Carrying Costs During Delay Are Material
keyFindings.detail: 18 months of carry on a $5.95M acquisition at 8% interest = ~$712K before construction even begins. This dead cost further erodes already-thin margins in the as-of-right scenario.
keyFindings.implication: Factor into all pro formas — this is not a rounding error

keyFindings.title: Adaptive Reuse Could Be the Smartest Play
keyFindings.detail: Preserving the 1843 Greek Revival and building new construction behind it (a) reduces community opposition, (b) may qualify for federal and state Historic Tax Credits (20%+), (c) creates a differentiated luxury product, and (d) shortens or avoids the demolition delay.
keyFindings.implication: Explore this strategy in detail — it may align financial and political incentives

keyFindings.title: The Seller Is Pricing in the Upside
keyFindings.detail: At $336/sf of buildable area, the asking price already captures most of the development value. The seller (Scott) appears aware of the lot's potential. This leaves insufficient margin for the developer who takes on 3+ years of execution risk.
keyFindings.implication: Any offer should be anchored to as-of-right economics, not speculative density

keyFindings.title: The Buyer Pool for Finished Product Is Deep
keyFindings.detail: Longwood Medical Area employs 50,000+ people (Harvard Medical, Dana-Farber, Brigham & Women's, Children's Hospital). Many are high-income professionals seeking walkable, transit-accessible housing. Brookline schools add family demand.
keyFindings.implication: Demand risk is low — the execution risk is in entitlement and construction, not sales

scenarios.condition: Seller won't negotiate below $5.5M AND 40B safe harbor is active
scenarios.reasoning: Insufficient margin at as-of-right density with no path to density upside

scenarios.condition: Seller negotiates to $4-4.5M AND as-of-right only
scenarios.reasoning: Margins of 8-12% achievable. Thin but workable for experienced developer. Adaptive reuse strategy preferred.

scenarios.condition: Seller negotiates to $4-4.5M AND 40B or MBTA overlay available
scenarios.reasoning: 19%+ margins with density upside. Strong location, deep buyer pool, manageable historic constraints.

scenarios.condition: Asking price ($5.95M) AND 40B comprehensive permit achievable (28+ units)
scenarios.reasoning: 12% margin at ask price with 40B. Negotiate to $5-5.25M for 15%+ returns.

scenarios.condition: Any price AND plan to hold for MBTA Act upzoning (2-5 year horizon)
scenarios.reasoning: Speculative land play. Regulatory tailwinds are real but timeline uncertain.

immediateNextSteps: Verify Brookline's Chapter 40B safe harbor status — this is the single most important variable
Check MBTA Communities Act Section 3A overlay maps for parcel coverage
Pull exact dimensional requirements from Brookline Zoning Table 5.01
Meet with Scott — express interest but anchor discussions at $4-4.5M based on as-of-right economics
Consult Brookline Planning Department for pre-application guidance
Engage a Brookline-experienced zoning attorney to assess entitlement pathways
